rubric.task_summary: Implement efficient prime check for n up to 10^7 in Python with correct edge case handling.
failure_modes: Incorrect primality logic for small numbers
Time limit exceeded for large inputs
Missing edge case handling for n <= 1

assumptions: Input n is a standard Python integer.
Performance requirement is strictly under 1 second for n up to 10,000,000.
known_risks: None significant; the 6k +/- 1 optimization is standard and efficient for this range.

assumptions: Input n is an integer.
The environment supports Python 3.
known_risks: None expected; the 6k +/- 1 optimization is standard and efficient for n up to 10^7.

assumptions: The input n will always be an integer.
Negative numbers are not prime.
0 and 1 are not prime.
2 and 3 are prime.
known_risks: For extremely large numbers beyond 10^7, this trial division method may become slow, but it is sufficient for the constraint of 10^7.
Very small negative inputs are handled correctly by the initial check.

assumptions: Input n is always an integer.
Performance requirement is strictly under 1 second for n up to 10,000,000.
known_risks: None significant; this is a standard optimized trial division algorithm.

assumptions: Input n will always be an integer.
The performance requirement of under 1 second for n up to 10,000,000 is achievable with an O(sqrt(n)) trial division algorithm.
known_risks: For extremely large numbers (much larger than 10^7), this algorithm might become slow, but it meets the specific constraint of 10^7.
Negative inputs are correctly handled as non-prime.

assumptions: The input n will always be an integer.
Performance requirement is for worst-case input n=10,000,000.
known_risks: None significant; this is a standard efficient trial division algorithm.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is measured on a standard modern CPU environment for a single call to the function with n = 10,000,000.
known_risks: Trial division is O(sqrt(n)), which is efficient for 10^7 but would be slow for much larger numbers (e.g., 10^15).
Python's interpreter speed might be a factor if the function were called millions of times in a loop, but for a single check of 10^7, it is well within 1 second.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is measured on a standard modern CPU environment executing Python 3.
The requirement for 'no I/O' means the function should not contain print() or input() calls.
known_risks: For extremely large numbers (much larger than 10^7), trial division becomes slow, but for the specified constraint of 10,000,000, O(sqrt(n)) is more than sufficient.
Python's overhead might be an issue if this were called millions of times in a loop, but as a standalone check for a single n up to 10^7, it is very fast.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The performance requirement of 1 second for n=10,000,000 refers to a single call to the function, which is easily satisfied by O(sqrt(n)) trial division.
known_risks: Very large prime numbers (though the constraint only goes to 10^7) would take longer, but 10^7 is well within the O(sqrt(n)) limits for Python.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is based on standard Python 3 interpreter performance for a single call or a reasonable number of calls up to 10^7.
known_risks: Trial division is O(sqrt(n)), which is efficient for a single check up to 10^7, but if the task implies checking *all* numbers up to 10^7 repeatedly, a sieve would be better. However, the signature implies a single number check.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is for a single call to the function with n=10,000,000 or a reasonable number of calls within that range.
known_risks: The trial division method (O(sqrt(n))) is efficient for single checks up to 10^7, but if the task implied a sieve for multiple queries, this would be slower than a precomputed sieve.
Python's overhead might approach the 1s limit if many large primes are checked sequentially, though for a single 10^7 check, it is extremely fast.

assumptions: The input n will be an integer.
Performance is measured against a single call for n = 10,000,000 or repeated calls within 1 second.
Standard Python 3 environment is used.
known_risks: For a single very large number (e.g., near 10^14), this O(sqrt(n)) approach might slow down, but for 10^7, it is extremely fast.
If the requirement implied checking *all* primes up to 10^7, a Sieve of Eratosthenes would be better, but for a function `is_prime(n)`, trial division is optimal for single queries.
